title: Na'vi Introductions
objective: Learn to greet others, introduce yourself, and ask how someone is doing.

data.text: Now that you know some words, let's learn how to use them in real situations. This lesson covers greetings — the phrases you need to start any conversation in Na'vi.
Na'vi greetings range from casual to deeply spiritual. Unlike English, where 'hello' works in almost any setting, Na'vi has different greetings for different relationships and moments.

data.description: The everyday greeting. Use it like English 'hi' or 'hello' — appropriate in most situations.
A warmer, friendlier version. The little word 'ma' is the vocative marker — use it whenever you're speaking directly to someone by name or title.
A blessing used when parting ways. Eywa is the Na'vi name for their planetary mother spirit. Closer to 'goodbye and bless you' than just 'bye'.

data.question: Which of these is the standard everyday greeting in Na'vi?

data.instruction: Listen to the greeting, then record yourself saying it.

data.english: How are you? (lit: 'Do you have well-being?')
data.description: The closest equivalent to 'how are you?'. Note that 'srak' at the end of any sentence turns it into a yes/no question.
The standard answer when someone asks how you are. Literally: 'to me there is well-being'.

data.instruction: Complete the response: someone has just asked you how you are.

data.description: The most famous Na'vi phrase. This isn't ordinary 'seeing' — it means 'I see into you, I understand who you are'. Used between people who deeply respect each other. Capitalized in English as 'See' to mark the difference from physical sight.

data.instruction: Listen and choose what this phrase means.
Match each phrase to its meaning.
The most famous Na'vi phrase. Listen, then record yourself.

data.question: What does the word 'ma' do in 'Kaltxì, ma 'eylan'?

options.text: It marks that you're addressing someone directly

data.text: 🎉 You can now greet a Na'vi speaker, ask how they're doing, respond when asked, and say goodbye with a blessing. That's enough to start a real conversation. You've finished the Fundamental level — the Intermediate lessons will start teaching you how Na'vi sentences are actually built.
